task: I’m trying to get a few household papers in order, and I want to make sure I’m not overlooking anything important before I file them away. Could you help me sort out what details are worth keeping together for things like bank papers, savings bonds, repair receipts, and any future paperwork that might matter later?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — keep it plain and practical.

For papers like that, I’d keep these details together:

- **What it is**  
  Bank statement, savings bond, repair receipt, warranty, etc.

- **Date**  
  When it was issued or when the work was done.

- **Name on it**  
  Person, account holder, business, or company.

- **Amount**  
  If there’s money involved.

- **Account or reference number**  
  Last 4 digits is usually enough for a note.

- **What it relates to**  
  Which house, car, appliance, account, or bond.

- **Where the original is filed**  
  Folder, box, binder section, or envelope.

- **Any follow-up needed**  
  Paid, unresolved, needs repair, needs renewal, or “keep until sold.”

For each item, I’d also note:

- **Keep / discard / unsure**
- **Why it matters**
- **How long to keep it**, if you know

A simple format could be:

- **Item:** Repair receipt for furnace  
- **Date:** 2024-11-03  
- **Name:** ABC Heating  
- **Amount:** $240  
- **Related to:** Furnace  
- **Filed with:** Home repairs folder  
- **Note:** Keep with warranty papers

For **savings bonds**, I’d be extra careful to keep together:

- Bond number
- Owner name
- Issue date
- Face value or purchase amount
- Where the bond is stored
- Any account or registration details
- Any note about whether it’s been cashed or transferred

For **bank papers**, keep:

- Bank name
- Account type
- Last 4 digits
- Statement date
- Whether it’s closed, active, or pending
- Any tax or fee notices
- Any unusual transaction notes

For **repair receipts**, keep:

- Business name
- Date of repair
- What was repaired
- Amount paid
- Warranty or guarantee terms
- Contact info if you may need them again

If you want, I can turn this into a very short checklist you could copy onto a page or index card.

user_simulator.text:
That’s useful, but I’d want it a little more exact for filing. The part about “where the original is filed” and “how long to keep it” is what I’d actually need, especially for the bonds and repair receipts.

Could you give me a simple keep-time guide too — like what to hold forever, what to keep 7 years, and what can go after a year or two? And maybe separate out what matters for:

- bank papers
- savings bonds
- house repair receipts
- tax-related papers
- anything tied to a will or estate

Plain list is fine. I don’t need a lecture, just something I can follow at the kitchen table.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s a plain keep-time guide you can use at the table.

## What to keep with each paper
For filing, keep these together:

- **What it is**
- **Date**
- **Who it’s from**
- **Who or what it’s about**
- **Original / copy / note**
- **Where the original is filed**
- **Keep time**
- **Any follow-up needed**

A short note like this is enough:

- **Repair receipt — furnace — 2024-11-03 — ABC Heating — original — filed in Home Repairs folder — keep 7 years**

---

## Simple keep-time guide

### Keep forever
Hold onto these unless you know for sure they can go:

- **Savings bonds**
- **Will**
- **Estate papers**
- **Deeds**
- **Titles**
- **Birth / marriage / death certificates**
- **Adoption papers**
- **Divorce papers**
- **Military discharge papers**
- **Old tax returns** if you want a full record, or if they go with estate matters
- **Any paper proving ownership, inheritance, or identity**

### Keep 7 years
A good rule for papers tied to money, taxes, or repairs:

- **Tax returns and supporting papers**
- **Bank statements** if they may matter for taxes, disputes, or proof of payment
- **Repair receipts**
- **Home improvement records**
- **Warranty papers**
- **Insurance claim papers**
- **Medical bills and records tied to taxes or claims**
- **Proof of payment**
- **Loan papers after payoff**, if you may need proof later

### Keep 1 to 2 years
These are usually short-term unless they connect to taxes, disputes, or ownership:

- **Routine bank notices**
- **Monthly statements you don’t need for tax records**
- **Paid utility bills**
- **Routine household receipts**
- **Routine service papers**
- **Catalogs, flyers, and general notices**
- **Temporary account papers**

### Can usually go sooner
If they’re not needed for taxes, proof, or ownership:

- **Duplicate copies**
- **Old envelopes**
- **Routine reminders**
- **Nonessential notes**
- **Expired offers**
- **Papers with no legal, money, or property value**

---

## By type

### Bank papers
Keep:

- Statements
- Deposit slips if needed as proof
- Closed account records
- Overdraft / fee notices
- Tax forms from the bank
- Anything showing payment, transfer, or account changes

**Keep time:**
- Regular statements: **1 to 2 years**, or **7 years** if they support taxes or disputes
- Closed account records: **7 years**
- Tax forms: **7 years**
- Proof of major transfers: **7 years** or longer if tied to estate or property

---

### Savings bonds
Keep:

- Bond certificate or registration record
- Bond number
- Owner name
- Issue date
- Purchase record
- Where stored
- Any transfer or redemption record

**Keep time:**
- **Forever** until cashed, transferred, or settled
- After cashing, keep the redemption record **7 years**
- If they are part of an estate, keep with estate papers **forever** or until the estate is fully settled

---

### House repair receipts
Keep:

- Receipt
- Work order
- Warranty
- Permit copy, if any
- Contractor name
- Date
- What was repaired
- Amount paid

**Keep time:**
- Small routine repairs: **1 to 2 years**
- Major repairs, appliance work, roof, furnace, plumbing, electrical: **7 years**
- Anything with a warranty: **keep for the warranty period, plus 1 year**
- Home improvements that affect taxes or sale value: **7 years or longer**

---

### Tax-related papers
Keep:

- Tax returns
- W-2s
- 1099s
- Receipts for deductions
- Charitable donation records
- Medical expense records if used for taxes
- Bank or investment records used for the return

**Keep time:**
- **7 years** is the safe plain rule
- If it supports a property sale, inheritance, or estate matter, keep longer

---

### Will or estate papers
Keep:

- Will
- Codicils
- Trust papers
- Probate papers
- Executor letters
- Inheritance records
- Beneficiary forms
- Asset lists
- Death certificates
- Final accountings
- Distribution records

**Keep time:**
- **Forever** for the will, trust papers, death certificates, and final estate records
- Keep anything showing who got what, and when, until the estate is fully closed
- If property or money is still being handled, do not discard it

---

## Quick filing note
If you want to keep it very plain, mark each paper with one of these:

- **Keep forever**
- **Keep 7 years**
- **Keep 2 years**
- **Keep until warranty ends**
- **Keep until estate is settled**

If you want, I can make this into a one-page checklist with blanks you can fill in by hand.

user_simulator.text:
That’s about right. Plain enough to use.

I’d keep the bond papers with the will stuff, all in one place, and the repair receipts in a separate home folder with the warranty papers clipped behind them. The 7-year rule seems sensible for anything tied to money or a house. 

What I needed was the simple part: what to keep together, and what not to toss too soon. You gave me that.
